metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS9: specify that the cohort field is added as an optional/nullable key in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar schema (expand-contract), document how consumers handle its absence (e.g., fort-wisp-wren treats missing cohort as a sentinel/default), and confirm no backfill of historical sidecars is required OR provide a resumable backfill plan if it is.
findings.finding: WS9 adds a 'cohort' field to the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar (a persisted output format), but the plan is silent on backward-compatibility handling: it does not state that the field is added as nullable/optional (expand-contract), does not specify how existing sidecar files lacking the cohort stamp are treated by downstream consumers (especially the dependent epic fort-wisp-wren), and provides no rollback or graceful-degradation story if a partially-stamped corpus of sidecars is left behind.
findings.location: Workstreams section — WS9 (sidecar cohort field); Integration ordering note: 'passes.py is edited by … WS9 (cohort stamps)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS9 story, add: (1) declare cohort as an optional field with a defined absent-value sentinel (e.g., null or omitted key); (2) state that fort-wisp-wren must handle missing-cohort gracefully (e.g., skip or assign to a default cohort) so old sidecars never cause failures; (3) if historical sidecars must be backfilled, add a one-time idempotent backfill script scoped to the sidecar directory.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Parent epic: Add WS11 to the docs/plan-review-gate.md integration ordering note alongside WS8 and WS10, specifying which sections WS11 edits and the landing order.
findings.finding: WS11 edits docs/plan-review-gate.md (latent-guard limitation + dual-of-A1 docs) but this file is absent from the parent's integration ordering section for that file, which lists only WS8 and WS10. This creates an unmanaged shared-file conflict risk.
findings.location: child f5d7-b2f2-1d70-4156
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent integration ordering: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md is edited by WS8 (MOVE_REGISTRY table ~148-160), WS10 (R-3 invariant + tuning checklist), WS11 (latent-guard limitation + dual-of-A1 note) -- coordinate all three; land WS8 -> WS10 -> WS11 (or verify sections are disjoint).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS11: Verify and cite the codebase location (passes.py or coach.py) that implements Pass-4 cross-criterion finding grouping, or clarify that 'grouped' means collocation in output order rather than active deduplication.
findings.finding: WS11 assumes that Pass-4's coaching pass groups findings from different criteria (E5 and removal-rationale) into a unified coach output, but neither WS11 nor any sibling verifies that this multi-criterion grouping mechanism exists or is currently implemented in the coaching pass.
findings.location: child f5d7-b2f2-1d70-4156
findings.suggested_fix: In WS11's What section, add: 'Verify that Pass-4 groups co-firing criteria (e.g. passes.py coach pass at line ~N); if no grouping exists, the AC proof should assert co-location in output order rather than structural grouping. Cite the mechanism.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS3: Resolve and document the overlay_routing value decision (reuse T10/T11's existing value OR add a new one) with a corresponding proof step in the AC.
findings.finding: WS3 leaves open a residual design decision: whether a new overlay_routing value is needed ('orchestrator.py route_criteria only if a new overlay_routing value is needed'), with no resolution or proof step confirming which path is taken — creating an undefined implementation branch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS3 AC: 'overlay_routing value decision recorded in code comment: [reuses existing llm overlay value / adds new value X with justification]. Proof: grep overlay_routing criteria_routing.json shows only the expected values.'

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS1: Name the specific sub-answers being added to the verifier prompts, and state explicitly whether each extends GRADED_BINARY (and thus is scored by Pass-3 validity) or is prompt-only guidance prose (not scored) — the two cases have different implementation paths.
findings.finding: WS1 'Pass-2 vocabulary expansion / new sub-answers' never specifies what the new sub-answers ARE or how they interact with the GRADED_BINARY tuple and verification_model(). The existing binary sub-question vocabulary is the GRADED_BINARY tuple in review_kernel/decide.py, and verification_model() derives its Binary Pydantic fields dynamically FROM that tuple — so adding a new graded sub-answer requires extending GRADED_BINARY (changing Pass-3 validity scoring) while a prompt-only prose addition is not scored at all. The plan states WS1 edits plan_review_verifier.md and _agentic.md with 'new sub-answers' but never names the sub-answers, their yes|no|insufficient|na field type, or whether they extend GRADED_BINARY. An executor implementing WS1 cannot determine from the plan whether to extend the GRADED_BINARY vocabulary (and thus alter Pass-3 arithmetic) or only add guidance prose.
findings.location: Integration ordering section: 'plan_review_verifier.md + _agentic.md are edited by WS1 (new sub-answers)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS1 child story, list each new sub-answer by name (e.g. 'hedge_detected: yes|no|insufficient|na'), declare whether it joins GRADED_BINARY or is prompt-guidance-only, and if it joins GRADED_BINARY note that verification_model() re-derives Binary from that tuple automatically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS3: Define the two new overlay criteria entries completely in the child story — criterion id, exec type, facet, applies_at (scope/suppress_when/suppress_types), block_threshold, default_posture, and at least the top-level checklist items.
findings.finding: WS3 'enumeration-overlay' adds 'two overlay criteria' as new entries in criteria_routing.json but the plan never specifies their criterion ids, exec type (1-TURN/2-STEP/AGENT), checklist vocabulary, applies_at scope, block_threshold, or default_posture. The existing criteria_routing.json has a fully defined per-entry schema (confirmed by reading the file); every current entry carries all required fields. The plan's only description is 'WS3 (two overlay criteria) — disjoint entries' in the integration ordering section. An executor cannot construct these entries from the plan text.
findings.location: Integration ordering section: 'criteria_routing.json is edited by WS2 (hedge 1-TURN + E4), WS3 (two overlay criteria) -- disjoint entries'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS9: Specify the cohort field's type (e.g. list[str] of criterion ids), which schema(s) it belongs to (P1 structured-output contract and/or sidecar payload), and whether it is LLM-emitted or deterministically stamped post-hoc by the orchestrator.
findings.finding: WS9 'cohort stamps' are described as additions to passes.py but the plan never specifies the cohort field's type, value vocabulary, or which output schema it belongs to. Specifically: the plan does not state whether cohort is (a) a new field in the plan_review_findings P1 structured-output contract (requiring a change to _pass1_model() and the prompt), (b) added to the sidecar build_payload() dict (no contract change), or (c) both. The sidecar.py build_payload() already has a defined slim() field selection; _pass1_model() defines the P1Output schema. Neither carries a cohort field today. The plan also does not define the field's type (e.g. sorted list[str] of criterion ids in the chunk).
findings.location: Integration ordering: 'passes.py is edited by WS7 (_resolve_system preamble), WS8 (MOVE_REGISTRY), WS9 (cohort stamps) -- disjoint functions'; Success Criteria: 'WS9 (cohort field) is the prerequisite for placeholder epic fort-wisp-wren'
findings.suggested_fix: In the WS9 child story, declare cohort as a deterministically-stamped field (the chunk's criterion ids, sorted) added to the orchestrator's post-processing of pass1_chunk output — not a new LLM-emitted field — and add it to the sidecar _slim() dict for downstream consumers. This avoids a P1 prompt change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in WS9's scope that the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar schema documentation (wherever it currently lives) is updated to include the new cohort field, its type, and its semantics, so fort-wisp-wren and future consumers have an accurate reference.
findings.finding: WS9 adds a new 'cohort field' to the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar schema, which is a schema change that any consumers of the sidecar (the placeholder epic fort-wisp-wren explicitly depends on it) will rely on, but no documentation update for the sidecar schema is called out in the plan.
findings.location: Workstreams section ('WS9 sidecar cohort field'); Integration ordering ('WS9 (cohort field) is the prerequisite for placeholder epic fort-wisp-wren')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For WS1/WS2/WS4's list-type rules (detection lexicons, verify-command blacklist), specify whether these live as configurable data (e.g., rubric YAML fields, a dedicated config section) or are embedded in prompt strings — and if the latter, justify why the tuning checklist in WS10 is sufficient mitigation.
findings.finding: WS4's verify-command blacklist and WS1/WS2's detection lexicons are new rules expected to evolve (the plan explicitly provides a 'tuning checklist' in WS10 docs), but the plan does not specify whether these lists will be configurable (e.g., data-driven config or rubric YAML) vs hardcoded in prompt strings — if hardcoded, routine lexicon/blacklist updates require prompt edits with associated CI risk.
findings.location: Workstreams section — WS1, WS2, WS4; Success Criteria — 'tuning checklist'
findings.suggested_fix: Declare in WS4 (and WS1/WS2) that list-type rules are stored in a named section of the criterion rubric file (e.g., a `blacklist:` YAML block) rather than inline prompt prose, so WS10's tuning checklist has a single authoritative location to document edits to.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS5: Enumerate specific recall/precision test cases for each named rubric addition (T3 axes/classes, G3 patterns, T5c, T11, E2, G4) in the AC, matching SC1's eval-guard requirement.
findings.finding: WS5's eval-guard coverage is vague for rubric additions beyond E6: the AC requires 'affected recall evals updated (incl. R-E6-cutover-no-live-ac)' but does not enumerate or mandate new recall/precision cases for T3 (three axes + four evidence classes), G3 (three contradiction patterns), T5c, T11, E2, and G4 consumer-enumeration additions — each of which changes reviewer output and therefore requires a case under SC1.
findings.suggested_fix: Add per-criterion AC lines: e.g. 'Recall case test_t3_contradicted_env: a plan with HTTP-only env contradicting OAuth flow scores Contradicted'; 'Recall case test_g3_scope_narrowing: a child covering narrower condition fires the scope-narrowing pattern'. One case per named addition suffices.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence scope summary and a pointer to the relevant gap-report section for each WS1–WS10 directly in the epic body (or confirm that each child story template already contains this, and link to a filled example).
findings.finding: WS1–WS10 are described only as a single compound label string ('WS1 Pass-2 vocabulary expansion; WS2 E4/exec:DET detectors; …') with no scope, content, or acceptance criteria specified; an executor creating child stories from these labels alone would need to consult an external document (the DSO gap report) whose contents are not reproduced or summarized in the plan.
findings.location: Workstreams section — 'WS1 Pass-2 vocabulary expansion; WS2 E4/exec:DET detectors; WS3 enumeration-overlay; WS4 DET verify-command blacklist; WS5 rubric enrichments; WS6 self-calibration anchors; WS7 prompt-hardening pass; WS8 coach moves; WS9 sidecar cohort field; WS10 docs + authoring surface'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile WS6's name across sections: decide whether its deliverable is 'self-calibration anchors' (the workstream list term) or 'blast_radius ratchet' (the integration-ordering term) or both, and use one consistent label.
findings.finding: WS6 is labeled 'self-calibration anchors' in the Workstreams section but 'blast_radius ratchet' in the Integration Ordering section — two different names for the same workstream's content create terminology drift that could confuse implementers about what WS6 actually delivers.
findings.location: Workstreams section ('WS6 self-calibration anchors') vs. Integration ordering section ('WS6 (blast_radius ratchet)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the Workstreams list entry: 'WS6 self-calibration anchors (incl. blast_radius ratchet)' to make clear both names refer to the same deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Parent/WS9 or WS10: Add an explicit AC or task for linking grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, and spin-cub-usher to this epic in the project tracker.
findings.finding: Parent AC2 ('Related design/placeholder epics linked: grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher') is not covered by any child story — no child creates or verifies these linkage actions.
findings.location: child 8656-92dc-f6eb-4a8f
findings.suggested_fix: Assign AC2 as an explicit task to either the epic owner directly or to WS9 (since fort-wisp-wren is its direct consumer) and WS10 (as the docs/surface workstream). Add a proof step: 'All four related epics appear as linked items in the tracker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC3 with a concrete, checkable outcome, e.g. 'Epic description contains links to all 11 child stories and all 4 related placeholder/design epics (grumpy-gland-rose, only-crave-art, fort-wisp-wren, spin-cub-usher).'
findings.finding: AC3 ('This epic is the durable home for the plan-review criteria-gap effort surfaced in this evaluation') is a governance/intent statement, not a falsifiable observable outcome — there is no deterministic in-session check that makes it true or false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3 with: 'Epic ticket body links to all 11 child stories (WS1–WS11) and all 4 related epics; the DSO gap report (dso_rebar_plan_review_gap_report.md) is referenced in the epic description.' This is checkable by inspection in one session.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split SC1 into (a) an in-session criterion scoped to this epic ('11 child stories created with design-decision content and eval-guard requirements documented in each') and (b) a release-gate criterion on the epic's completion tracker ('all 11 child stories closed with eval-guard evidence').
findings.finding: Success Criterion 1 describes the COMPLETED state of all 11 child workstreams ('implemented and eval-guarded'), which cannot be completed in one working session on this coordination epic — it is effectively the release-completion criterion for a multi-story epic, not an in-session observable for the epic itself.
findings.location: Success Criteria — first bullet ('Each of the 11 adopted workstreams … is implemented and eval-guarded')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace SC1 with: 'Each child story WS1–WS11 is created with: (a) scope and design decisions captured, (b) the eval-guard requirement (recall/precision case OR deterministic unit check) specified in the story body.' Move 'implemented and eval-guarded' to a separate program-completion checklist on the epic, not a Success Criterion for this epic's session work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit posture statement for WS11 in either the Workstreams section or Success Criteria (e.g., 'WS11 ships advisory-by-default; blocking posture requires calibration evidence per SC3') so SC3 is coverable.
findings.finding: WS11 is a new criterion that alters reviewer output (triggering SC1's eval-guard requirement and SC3's 'no new blocking behavior without calibration evidence' constraint), yet the plan body contains no description of WS11's gate posture (advisory vs. blocking). SC3 is therefore potentially uncovered — a success criterion with no corresponding described behavior to verify against.
findings.location: Success Criteria SC3 ('No new blocking behavior ships without calibration evidence') vs. Workstreams section ('WS11 removal-rationale criterion (Chesterton's Fence, removal-side dual of A1)')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the WS11 workstream entry: '(advisory-by-default per SC3; blocking posture requires calibration data before graduation)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for behavioral changes to passes.py and verifier prompt files (e.g., feature-flag the preamble addition, or document the revert commit sequence per WS landing), so the CI gate failure has a defined recovery path, not just a detection path.
findings.finding: The plan edits passes.py (_resolve_system preamble in WS7, MOVE_REGISTRY in WS8, cohort stamps in WS9) and verifier prompts (WS1/WS6/WS7) — behavioral changes to the live reviewer — but provides no rollback/back-out path if the drift-parity gate fails or a regression is detected post-land. Integration ordering is specified, but ordering ≠ reversibility.
findings.location: Integration ordering section; Success Criteria ('Prompt/routing CI drift-parity gate stays green after rubric edits')
findings.suggested_fix: For each WS that edits passes.py or a verifier prompt, add a one-line back-out note: 'Revert: revert commit <WS-tag> to restore prior behavior; no data migration needed.' Alternatively, land WS7's preamble change behind a config flag (e.g., PROMPT_HARDENING_ENABLED=true) so it can be toggled off without a revert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add WS11 to the list of workstreams that update docs/plan-review-gate.md (or a new ADR), capturing the Chesterton's Fence criterion's rationale, scope, and relationship to A1, so the architectural decision is durably documented and not trapped only in a child story.
findings.finding: WS11 introduces a net-new architectural criterion (Chesterton's Fence / removal-rationale as a plan-review criterion) that is explicitly called out as 'NOT in the DSO report' and a design decision — a new architectural contract — but no ADR or dedicated doc target is specified; only a child story is mentioned.
findings.location: Scope note ('WS11 removal-rationale criterion (Chesterton's Fence, removal-side dual of A1) -- NET-NEW, not from the DSO report'); Success Criteria ('11 child stories created')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to the WS11 scope: 'Update docs/plan-review-gate.md criteria table to add the removal-rationale criterion row (analogous to the A1 row), including rationale and the design decision to treat it as a dual of A1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit documentation artifact for WS11's design decision (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or a named section in docs/plan-review-gate.md) capturing why Chesterton's Fence is criterion-local, its relationship to A1, and that it is net-new (not DSO-sourced).
findings.finding: WS11 introduces a NET-NEW criterion paradigm (Chesterton's Fence / removal-rationale as a dual of A1) — a novel architectural decision in the review rubric — but the plan does not call out an explicit ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry for WS11 specifically; WS10's doc scope covers R-3 invariant and tuning checklist, not the WS11 criterion-design rationale.
findings.location: Workstreams section — WS11; 'Integration ordering' — docs/plan-review-gate.md scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Caller impact' section (or a note per affected WS) enumerating known consumers of passes.py, criteria_routing.json, and plan_review_verifier.md, and specify how each WS's changes preserve or explicitly migrate those consumers' contracts.
findings.finding: The plan edits shared infrastructure files (passes.py, criteria_routing.json, plan_review_verifier.md, docs/plan-review-gate.md) that are consumed by existing callers (CI pipelines, engineers running the gate, any tooling invoking Pass-2/Pass-4 behavior), but nowhere acknowledges those callers or how behavioral changes to the gate will be communicated or validated against them.
findings.location: Integration ordering section / Success Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the Integration ordering section, add a brief table: for each edited shared file, list (a) known callers/consumers, (b) whether the WS change is additive-only or breaks existing shape, and (c) the verification step (e.g., 'existing eval-spec run confirms no regression', 'CI passes.py import test').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS8: Name the new coach moves being added to MOVE_REGISTRY — provide at minimum the move id, name, and a draft template string for each — so the deliverable is reviewable at the epic level.
findings.finding: WS8 'MOVE_REGISTRY additions' edits the MOVE_REGISTRY in passes.py but the plan never names which move ids are added, their move names, or their template strings. The existing MOVE_REGISTRY (passes.py lines 686–731) contains moves 1–9, 11, 12 — move id 10 is visibly absent. The locked-template constraint (every template must contain a single {subject} placeholder, enforced by validate_move_registry) is a structural requirement an implementer must satisfy. The plan gives no content for the new moves.
findings.location: Integration ordering: 'passes.py is edited by WS7 (_resolve_system preamble), WS8 (MOVE_REGISTRY), WS9 (cohort stamps) -- disjoint functions'; Workstreams: 'WS8 coach moves'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS3: Declare G-5 and G-10 default_posture advisory in criteria_routing.json entries and add a proof step confirming verdicts are non-blocking.
findings.finding: WS3 (G-5 and G-10 overlay criteria) does not state a posture (advisory vs. blocking) for the two new criteria, leaving SC3 ('no new blocking behavior ships without calibration evidence, advisory-by-default') unverified for this child.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS3 AC: 'G-5 and G-10 registered with default_posture: advisory (or equivalent non-blocking flag). Proof: verdict on a triggering plan is advisory, not block.' Mirror the WS11 advisory-posture AC pattern.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
